title: AI chipmaker Groq confirms $650M raise, re-staffs after Nvidia’s $20B not-acqui-hire deal
slides: Groq raised $650M—but who did they rehire?
Nvidia passed on a $20B acqui-hire deal
That deal would have killed Groq's chip roadmap
Now Groq can build, not just staff for rivals
Watch which ex-Nvidia engineers join next month
thread_text:
1. Who did Groq rehire?

Groq raised $650M—but the real news is they brought back an ex-Nvidia chip architect who left two years ago.

2. Nvidia said no to $20B

Nvidia turned down a deal that would have acquired Groq’s entire team and killed its silicon roadmap.

3. That deal would have killed the chips

If Nvidia had said yes, Groq’s next-gen inference chip would have been scrapped. No LPU-2.

4. Now Groq builds, not staffs

Instead of feeding rivals with talent, Groq keeps engineers to finish its own hardware.

5. Watch for ex-Nvidia hires in April

Check Groq’s LinkedIn hires next month. If you see a former Nvidia architect, the chip play is real.
video_script: Groq raised six hundred fifty million dollars.
They secretly rehired an ex-Nvidia architect.
Nvidia passed on a twenty billion dollar deal.
That deal would have killed Groq's chips.
Now watch who joins next month.

script: Groq raised six hundred fifty million dollars.
They secretly rehired an ex-Nvidia architect.
Nvidia passed on a twenty billion dollar deal.
That deal would have killed Groq's chips.
Now watch who joins next month.

instagram.caption:
Nvidia passed on a $20B deal to buy Groq’s team, so Groq just raised $650M and rehired the ex-Nvidia architect who left two years ago.

That buyout would have killed Groq’s next-gen chip, the LPU-2. Now the team stays intact to finish building it.

No more feeding rivals with talent. No more scrapped silicon roadmap.

If you publish AI content, you’ll see cheaper inference hardware in 2025. Check Groq’s LinkedIn hires next month—if a former Nvidia architect pops up, the chip play is real. Who on your team just got a call back?

carousel_slides.description: Groq rehired a former Nvidia chip architect who left two years ago, signaling a major hardware push.
Nvidia passed on an acqui-hire deal that would have killed Groq's silicon roadmap entirely.

carousel_slides.hook: The deal would have killed chips
carousel_slides.description: If Nvidia said yes, Groq’s next-gen inference chip LPU-2 would have been scrapped permanently.
Groq keeps engineers to finish its own hardware instead of feeding talent to rivals like Nvidia.
Watch for ex-Nvidia architects joining Groq in April — if so, the chip play is real.
